title: Berlin & Germany
dek: Cold-war scars turned creative capital — street food, world-class museums, and day trips to Prussian palaces.
footer: Location photos via Wikimedia Commons — tap any credit for its source, author and licence.
verified: ✓ Verified June 2026 — reconfirm prices, hours & transport fares before you go
intro: Berlin rewards walkers and cyclists: most of the major sights are close enough to string together on foot, the transit system is comprehensive, and the food scene runs from €2 currywurst to Michelin-starred tasting menus.

sections.title: When you land
sections.body: Berlin Brandenburg Airport (BER) has two options for getting in:
checklist: FEX Airport Express — fastest option; runs every 15 min (04:00–01:00), reaches Hauptbahnhof in ~23 min and Potsdamer Platz in ~19 min. Uses a standard ABC single ticket (€5.00) — buy at the airport machines before boarding
S-Bahn S9 or S45 — slower (~45–55 min to central stations) but more frequent; same ABC ticket (€5.00)
Taxi or Uber to the centre runs ≈€45–70 and takes 30–50 min (longer in traffic). Uber, Bolt and FreeNow are usually 10–20% cheaper than metered taxis
Validate your ticket at the yellow (BVG) or red (S-Bahn) machines before your first trip — inspectors board regularly and fines are steep

sections.title: Local essentials
sections.body: Plug: Type F (Schuko, two round pins), 230 V / 50 Hz — US devices need an adapter and a voltage check. Time zone: CET/CEST (UTC+1 in winter, UTC+2 in summer — one hour ahead of the UK). Tap water: safe and excellent throughout Germany — carry a reusable bottle. Sundays: most shops are closed; do your grocery shopping on Saturday. Pfand (bottle deposit): cans and bottles carry a €0.25 deposit — return them at any supermarket machine to get it back. Pharmacies show a red 'A'; a duty rotation (Notdienstapotheke) keeps one open 24/7 nearby — check aponet.de or the notice in any pharmacy window.

sections.title: Entry & documents
sections.body: ⚠️ The rules below assume a US passport. Other nationalities should check their own requirements.
checklist: Visa-free for US citizens — up to 90 days in any 180-day period across the Schengen area
Passport must be valid for at least 3 months beyond your departure date and issued within the last 10 years
ETIAS not yet required as of June 2026, but is expected to launch in late 2026 — check before you travel (≈€20 fee when live)
EU Type F plug adapter — one power strip handles everything
Travel insurance covering medical care abroad — strongly recommended

sections.title: Booking checklist
sections.body: Roughly in this order — the Reichstag dome is free but books out weeks ahead:
checklist: Reichstag dome — free, but register on bundestag.de several weeks in advance; fills fast in summer
Flights, round-trip — ≈$600–1,200 from the US East Coast (book early)
Accommodation — book 4–6 weeks ahead in summer; budget hotel ≈€90–130/night
Museum Island / Pergamon area — check current closures (Pergamon Altar hall closed until ≈2027); consider the Museum Pass Berlin (3 days, 30+ museums) ≈€32
Sanssouci Palace, Potsdam (day trip) — timed entry recommended, especially on weekends
Travel insurance (a few weeks out)
Offline maps + BVG app for transit (week before)

sections.title: Phone & data
sections.body: Germany is in the EU, so any EU-roaming SIM works at no extra charge. US travellers: eSIM from Airalo, Holafly or similar (≈$5–15 for 5–10 GB) is the easiest option; alternatively, local SIMs from Telekom, Vodafone DE or O2 are available at the airport and in city shops. Coverage in Berlin and Potsdam is excellent; rural areas can be patchy. The BVG app (transit) and Google Maps work reliably across the city.

sections.title: Holidays & your dates
sections.body: No national public holidays fall in Berlin during June, July or August 2026 — museums, shops and transit run on normal schedules all summer. The next national holiday is German Unity Day on October 3. Note that some holidays (Assumption, Corpus Christi) exist only in certain states — they don't apply in Berlin. Sunday closing laws are the main thing to plan around: almost all shops are closed on Sundays, so stock up on food and supplies on Saturday. Berlin is permitted up to 8 exception Sundays per year where some shops may open — check locally if you're there on a Sunday.

sections.title: Money & currency
sections.body: Germany uses the euro (€). Mid-2026 rate: €1 ≈ $1.16 (approximate — check before you go). Germany is more cash-dependent than most of Western Europe — many small restaurants, street-food stalls, independent shops and markets are cash-only. Always carry €20–40 in cash. Cards (especially Mastercard/Visa) are accepted at hotels, chains and larger restaurants, but don't assume. Tipping: not compulsory, but 10% or rounding up is standard at sit-down restaurants — hand the cash or card to the server and state the total you want to pay (e.g. 'Machen Sie 48, bitte' on a €44 bill); do not leave money on the table.

sections.title: Budget & daily costs
sections.intro: Rough per-person, mid-range estimates in euros for a 7-day Berlin trip. Berlin is one of Europe's better-value capitals — street food is cheap, many sights are free or low-cost, and the Museum Pass covers a lot. Tap a field to log your real spend; the total saves on this device.

items.label: Accommodation, per night
items.note: Budget hotel ≈€90–130; hostel dorm from ≈€30–45. Book ahead for summer.

items.label: Food & drink, per day
items.note: Döner or currywurst ≈€5–8; sit-down lunch ≈€10–14; dinner ≈€15–25. Supermarkets (Aldi, Netto, Lidl) save money on breakfast and snacks.

items.label: Local transport, per day
items.note: 24-hour AB day pass = €11.20; ABC (covers BER airport + Potsdam) = €12.90.

items.label: Sights & activities, per day
items.note: Reichstag dome free (book ahead). Individual museums ≈€6–14. Museum Pass Berlin (3 days, 30+ sites) ≈€32 — good value if you're doing Museum Island.

items.label: Flights — round trip
items.note: ≈$600–1,200 USD from the US East Coast — varies widely by date and carrier.

items.label: Potsdam day trip (Sanssouci)
items.note: ABC day pass ≈€12.90 covers all transit + Sanssouci Palace entry ≈€14 (≈check current price). Book timed entry in advance.

sections.title: Health & pharmacy
sections.body:
Emergencies: dial 112 (free) for ambulance or fire; 110 for police. For non-emergency medical help outside office hours, call 116 117 — Germany's 24/7 medical on-call line, English operators available.

Pharmacies (Apotheken) show a red 'A'. A duty rotation (Notdienstapotheke) keeps at least one open 24/7 in each district — check aponet.de or the notice posted in any pharmacy window for the current duty pharmacy. An after-hours surcharge of ≈€2.50 is set by law. Tap water is safe to drink throughout Germany — but never use hot tap water for drinking or cooking (Legionella risk, especially in older buildings). Travel insurance covering medical care is strongly recommended for US visitors.
checklist: Save 112 (ambulance/fire) and 110 (police)
Save 116 117 for non-emergency medical advice (24/7)
Confirm travel insurance covers medical treatment abroad
Pack medicines in original packaging, plus copies of prescriptions
Find duty pharmacy at aponet.de if you need one after hours

sections.title: Etiquette & language
sections.body:
English is widely spoken in Berlin — tourist areas, hotels, restaurants and museums present no language barrier. Outside Berlin, fluency drops; a few phrases go a long way: Hallo (hello), Guten Tag (good day), Danke schön (thank you), Bitte (please / you're welcome), Entschuldigung (excuse me), Wo ist…? (where is…?), Die Rechnung, bitte (the bill, please), Sprechen Sie Englisch? (do you speak English?).

A few practical habits: carry cash — many places don't take cards. Tip by stating your total to the server, not by leaving money on the table. Don't jaywalk conspicuously — locals wait for the green pedestrian light and will let you know if you don't. Sunday is quiet — shops are closed; plan accordingly. Return your bottles at supermarket Pfand machines. Recycling is taken seriously — bins are colour-coded and segregated.

sections.title: Key transit routes
steps: Airport → centre: FEX Airport Express every 15 min — Potsdamer Platz in ~19 min, Hauptbahnhof in ~23 min. Standard ABC single ticket €5.00. Validate before boarding. (S9/S45 is slower — ~45–55 min — but same ticket.)

Around Berlin: The U-Bahn (underground, yellow signage) and S-Bahn (surface/elevated, green signage) share the same ticket. AB day pass €11.20 covers all buses, trams, U-Bahn and S-Bahn within the city ring. Validate once — it's valid for 2 hours on a single, or until 3 a.m. the next day on a day pass.
Brandenburg Gate / Mitte sights: U-Bahn U55 to Brandenburger Tor, or S-Bahn S1/S2/S25 to Unter den Linden / Brandenburger Tor. The whole central historic mile is walkable.
Museum Island: S-Bahn S3/S5/S7/S9 to Hackescher Markt, then a short walk; or tram M4/M5/M6 to Lustgarten.
East Side Gallery: S-Bahn S3/S5/S7/S9 to Ostbahnhof, then a 5-min walk along the river.

To Potsdam (day trip): RE1 Regional Express from Berlin Hbf or Ostbahnhof — 25 min to Potsdam Hbf. ABC day pass (€12.90) covers the whole journey. Sanssouci Park is a 30-min walk or short bus from the station.
Cycling: Berlin has 620+ km of dedicated lanes. Daily rental ≈€10–20 from Dott, Nextbike or local shops — a good option for Tiergarten and the central sights.

items.title: Arrival + Mitte first look
items.body: FEX from the airport to Potsdamer Platz or Hauptbahnhof. Check in, then walk the central mile: Brandenburg Gate, the Holocaust Memorial, Pariser Platz. Evening on Unter den Linden.
checklist: ABC ticket validated before leaving airport
Carry €20–40 cash from the start

items.title: Reichstag + Tiergarten + Holocaust Memorial
items.body: Morning at the Reichstag dome (free — you need a booking from bundestag.de). Walk through Tiergarten to the Victory Column. Afternoon at the Memorial to the Murdered Jews of Europe — moving and worth taking time over. Evening meal in the government quarter area.
checklist: Reichstag booking confirmed (bring ID — passport or driver's licence)
Memorial has no entry fee; underground exhibition ≈€5

items.title: Museum Island
items.body: The five UNESCO museums on a river island. Highlights: the Neues Museum (Egyptian collection, Nefertiti bust), the Altes Museum (Greek and Roman antiquities), and the Berliner Dom (cathedral) exterior and roof. ⚠️ The Pergamon Altar hall is closed until approximately 2027 — check current openings before you go. Museum Pass Berlin (≈€32) covers all five for 3 days.
items.note: Pergamon Altar hall closed for renovation — confirm what's open at smb.museum.
checklist: Museum Pass Berlin worth buying if you plan 3+ museums
Berliner Dom entry ≈€9; roof access included

items.title: East Side Gallery + Kreuzberg
items.body: Morning at the East Side Gallery — 1.3 km of original Berlin Wall painted by international artists; free, outdoors. Lunch in Kreuzberg (best Döner in the city is contested but Kreuzberg is the place to try). Afternoon: Tempelhof Field (the former airport turned public park) if you want open space.
checklist: East Side Gallery is free and always open
Bring cash for street food in Kreuzberg

items.title: Potsdam day trip — Sanssouci
items.pace: Full day — hilly park; wear good shoes
items.body: RE1 from Berlin Hbf to Potsdam Hbf (25 min). Bus or 30-min walk to Sanssouci Palace, Frederick the Great's rococo summer palace set in 500-hectare terraced gardens. Book a timed palace entry online — ≈€14. Also worth: the adjacent Orangery and Charlottenhof. Return to Berlin in the evening.
items.note: Sanssouci is the most visited sight in Germany outside Berlin — book online, especially on weekends.
checklist: Sanssouci timed entry booked
ABC day pass covers all transit there and back
The park itself is free; palace entry is ticketed

items.title: Charlottenburg + neighbourhood day
items.body: Morning at Charlottenburg Palace (Berlin's largest palace, ≈€12 — or just the free gardens). Afternoon in one of Berlin's neighbourhood markets: Mauerpark flea market (Sunday mornings, free entry) or Boxhagener Platz market. Evening: KaDeWe department store food hall — the largest in continental Europe — for a splurge.
checklist: Mauerpark market is Sunday only — adjust itinerary if this is a weekday
KaDeWe food hall is open Mon–Sat

items.title: Departure day
items.body: Last currywurst or Frühstück (German bakery breakfast). FEX from any central station to BER — about 19–23 min. Allow 2.5 hours before your flight for international departures; security at BER can have queues.
checklist: ABC ticket for the FEX (€5.00) — validate before boarding
Allow 2.5 hr before international departure

items.name: Brandenburger Tor
items.body: The 18th-century neoclassical gate that divided East and West for 28 years — now the city's centrepiece. Free to view at any hour; spectacular at dusk. Pariser Platz around it is pedestrianised.

items.name: Reichstag & glass dome
items.body: The German parliament building, topped by Norman Foster's glass dome with a 360° rooftop walkway and views over the government quarter. Entry is free but requires advance registration on bundestag.de — book several weeks ahead in summer. Bring your passport.

items.name: East Side Gallery
items.body: The longest remaining section of the Berlin Wall, covered in murals painted by international artists in 1990. Free, open-air, always accessible. Dmitri Vrubel's 'Fraternal Kiss' (Brezhnev and Honecker) is the most photographed image.

img.alt: East Side Gallery murals, Berlin Wall

items.name: Museum Island (Museumsinsel)
items.kicker: Five UNESCO museums on a river island
items.body: The Neues Museum (Nefertiti bust), Altes Museum (Greek and Roman antiquities), Bode Museum and Alte Nationalgalerie. ⚠️ The Pergamon Altar hall is closed for renovation until approximately 2027 — check smb.museum before you go. Museum Pass Berlin (≈€32) covers all five for 3 days.

img.alt: Berliner Dom (Berlin Cathedral) on Museum Island

items.name: Memorial to the Murdered Jews of Europe
items.body: Peter Eisenman's field of 2,711 grey concrete slabs of varying heights, metres from the Brandenburg Gate. Free to walk through at any time. The underground exhibition (≈€5) provides historical context — worth the time.

items.name: Checkpoint Charlie
items.kicker: The Cold War's most famous crossing
items.body: The former US Army crossing point between East and West Berlin. The replica guardhouse and signage are free to see; the Checkpoint Charlie Museum next door charges entry (≈€15 — check current price). Busy and tourist-heavy, but historically resonant.

sections.title: Free Berlin
sections.body: A lot of Berlin's best things cost nothing. The Brandenburg Gate, East Side Gallery, Holocaust Memorial (exterior), Tiergarten, Tempelhofer Feld (vast former airport turned park — one of the most unusual spaces in any city) and Mauerpark are all free. The Reichstag dome is free with an advance booking. Many state museums have free entry on the first Sunday of the month — check smb.museum for the current offer.

items.name: Schloss Sanssouci, Potsdam
items.body: Frederick the Great's intimate rococo summer palace set in 500 hectares of terraced gardens, fountains and smaller palaces. UNESCO World Heritage. Palace entry ≈€14 — book timed tickets online, especially at weekends. RE1 train from Berlin Hbf (25 min); ABC day pass (€12.90) covers the journey.

img.alt: Sanssouci Palace with vineyard terraces, Potsdam

sections.title: Sachsenhausen Memorial
sections.body: Sachsenhausen concentration camp memorial is 35 km north of Berlin — about 50 minutes by S-Bahn S1 to Oranienburg, then a 20-minute walk. Open daily (closed Mondays November–March); entry is free, with a suggested donation of ≈€3 for maintenance. Guided tours from Berlin are also available. A sobering but important half-day; pair it with a lighter afternoon in Berlin, not back-to-back with other heavy history.

sections.title: What to eat
sections.body:
Berlin's street food is its best and cheapest. Currywurst (sliced pork sausage with curried ketchup) is the city's signature snack — ≈€3–5 from any Imbiss stand; Curry 36 in Kreuzberg is the most famous. Döner kebab was essentially invented in Berlin's Turkish community — the city's best is a genuine debate, but Kreuzberg and Neukölln are the neighbourhoods to try. Pretzel (Brezel) with butter from a bakery makes a cheap breakfast alongside strong coffee (≈€3–4 total).

For a proper sit-down meal, Schnitzel (breaded cutlet), Sauerbraten (slow-braised beef) and Flammkuchen (Alsatian flatbread) are reliable and filling. Beer is taken seriously — ask for a Helles (lager), Weizen (wheat beer) or a local craft option. Water at restaurants: ask for Leitungswasser (tap water) — it's free and perfectly safe; still water is stilles Wasser, sparkling is Sprudelwasser.

sections.title: Shopping
sections.body: Kurfürstendamm (Ku'damm) is Berlin's main shopping boulevard — international brands and the iconic KaDeWe department store (food hall on the top floor is worth visiting even without buying). Hackescher Markt and the surrounding Mitte courtyards (Höfe) have independent boutiques and design shops. Mauerpark flea market (Sunday mornings, Prenzlauer Berg) is one of Berlin's best markets — vintage clothes, records and second-hand finds. Sunday closing applies everywhere: plan shopping for weekdays or Saturday.

sections.title: Souvenirs worth bringing home
items: A piece of original Berlin Wall with certificate — sold at legitimate museum shops
Ampelmännchen merchandise (the beloved East German crossing-light figure)
German chocolate or marzipan (Niederegger for serious marzipan)
A bottle of Riesling or Spätburgunder (Pinot Noir) from a wine shop
A Pfand bottle as a souvenir — return it for €0.25, or keep it
Vinyl records — Berlin's record shops (especially in Kreuzberg and Mitte) are exceptional
